w:r.w:t: Technical Architecture for High-Fidelity Parsing of Japanese Office Open XML (DOCX) Documents for LLM Ingestion

The ingestion of unstructured data into Large Language Models (LLMs) has shifted the focus of document parsing from simple text extraction to high-fidelity semantic reconstruction. In the context of enterprise retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and corpus training, the precision with which a parser handles document layout, typography, and embedded metadata directly correlates to the quality of the model's downstream reasoning. Among the various file formats encountered in professional environments, the Office Open XML (OOXML) WordprocessingML format—colloquially known as DOCX—presents a unique set of challenges due to its extensive use of indirection, complex inheritance models, and historical accumulation of features meant to preserve binary compatibility with legacy systems.

This report provides an exhaustive technical specification for engineering a custom, highly scalable Python-based parser for DOCX files. While generic parsers exist, they typically rely on Document Object Model (DOM) approaches that load entire file structures into memory, rendering them inefficient for high-throughput pipelines processing massive document archives. Furthermore, standard open-source libraries frequently fail to correctly interpret East Asian typographic features such as Ruby annotations (Furigana) and vertical text layout (Tategaki), resulting in tokenization noise and semantic degradation for Japanese-language corpora.

The proposed architecture adopts a "build-from-scratch" strategy utilizing Python’s zipfile and xml.etree.ElementTree.iterparse modules. This approach ensures O(1) memory complexity relative to document size and allows for granular control over the linearization of non-linear text structures. The report details the internal mechanics of the Open Packaging Conventions (OPC), the algorithmic resolution of content stored across disjoint XML parts, and specific logic for handling Japanese layout semantics, image accessibility data, and complex field codes, while strictly enforcing the deprecation of content marked with static strikethrough to maintain data hygiene for LLM consumption.
2. The Open Packaging Conventions (OPC) and Internal Package Structure

To accurately parse a .docx file, one must first recognize that it is not a single file but a compressed archive structured according to the Open Packaging Conventions (OPC), standardized as ISO/IEC 29500-2.
This container format creates a logical filesystem within the archive, decoupling the storage of content (text, images, metadata) into discrete "Parts" connected by "Relationships."

The parser’s entry point is the physical ZIP archive. Unlike legacy binary formats (DOC), which used the Object Linking and Embedding (OLE) structured storage, OOXML uses standard ZIP compression. This accessibility allows for the inspection of internal components without specialized API calls, but it also mandates that a robust parser handles the archive strictly as a file system wrapper. Python’s zipfile module provides the necessary interface to read these components as streams, which is critical for the scalability requirement; extracting files to a temporary disk location incurs unnecessary I/O overhead and should be avoided in favor of in-memory streaming.

A common implementation error in bespoke parsers is assuming fixed file paths (e.g., assuming the main text is always at word/document.xml). While this is the default behavior of Microsoft Word, the standard allows parts to be named arbitrarily. The only reliable mechanism to locate the main document is through the .xml file located at the root of the archive.

This file acts as a registry for the package, mapping file extensions or specific part names to MIME types. The parser must ingest this XML map to identify the part with the content type application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document.main+xml. This content type serves as the definitive identifier for the entry point of the parsing graph, regardless of the physical filename within the ZIP structure.
OOXML utilizes a graph-based architecture where parts reference each other via relationships rather than direct file paths. This indirection is managed through .rels files.

For every part in the package (e.g., document.xml), there exists an optional corresponding relationship part (e.g., _rels/document.xml.rels). This XML file contains a list of <Relationship> elements, each defining an ID (e.g., rId1), a Type (e.g., http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/officeDocument/2006/relationships/image), and a Target (e.g., media/image1.png).

When the parser encounters an element in the main document, such as an image reference <a:blip r:embed="rId1"/> or a hyperlink <w:hyperlink r:id="rId5">, it does not contain the data or the URI. Instead, it holds a reference key (rId1, rId5). The parser must consult the loaded relationship map to resolve rId1 to word/media/image1.png. Failure to implement a robust relationship resolver will result in a parser that extracts text but fails to link it to its associated semantic assets like images, numbering definitions, or hyperlinks.
3. Scalable Parsing Architecture: The Iterparse Strategy

The requirement for high scalability dictates the rejection of Document Object Model (DOM) approaches, such as those used by python-docx or xml.dom.minidom. These libraries construct the entire XML tree in Random Access Memory (RAM) before processing, which leads to memory consumption scaling linearly (or super-linearly) with file size. For large documents or high-concurrency ingestion pipelines, this creates a bottleneck.

The solution is to utilize the iterparse method from Python’s xml.etree.ElementTree or lxml.etree libraries. This method processes the XML file as a sequential stream of events—specifically start (when an opening tag is encountered) and end (when a closing tag is encountered).

The operational efficiency of iterparse relies on the immediate release of memory. Once an element has been processed and its relevant data extracted (typically at the end event), the parser must explicitly call the .clear() method on the element. This action removes the element’s content from memory. However, a technical nuance often overlooked is that the root element maintains references to cleared children, which can still lead to memory bloat ("root bloat"). Therefore, the parser must also periodically clear references from the root element to its children to ensure true O(1) memory usage relative to the document length.

A significant challenge with stream parsing is the loss of hierarchical context. In a DOM tree, one can traverse upwards (element.parent) to check if a text run is inside a table, a footnote, or a deleted section. In a linear stream, this "parent" knowledge is absent unless explicitly tracked.
To bridge this gap, the parser must implement a custom

When a start event is triggered for a container element (e.g., <w:tbl>, <w:p>, <w:r>, <w:ruby>), a state object representing that container is pushed onto a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) stack. This object holds metadata specific to that scope (e.g., "inside a table cell," "inside a strikethrough run").
When the corresponding end event is triggered, the stack is popped, returning the parser to the previous state.

When a text node (<w:t>) is encountered, the parser inspects the current tip of the stack to determine how to treat the text—whether to append it to a paragraph buffer, treat it as a ruby annotation, or discard it because it sits inside a <w:del> (tracked deletion) block.

OOXML is heavily namespaced, utilizing URIs such as http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/wordprocessingml/2006/main (often mapped to prefix w) and http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/drawingml/2006/main (mapped to a or wp). The iterparse function returns tags with their full namespaced URI in curly braces (e.g., {http://schemas...}p). A robust parser must implement a namespace normalization layer that either strips these URIs or maps them to constant short-names to prevent code verbosity and fragility against namespace prefix changes in different editor versions.

The core objective is to extract semantic text while filtering out noise. In the context of LLM ingestion, "noise" includes not only formatting tags but also content that has been logically deprecated by the author.
The atomic unit of text in WordprocessingML is the Run (<w:r>). A Paragraph (<w:p>) is a sequence of runs. The text content itself is contained within Text elements (<w:t>) inside the run.

The <w:t> element may include the xml:space="preserve" attribute. The parser must respect this; if present, leading and trailing spaces in the text string are significant. If absent, the standard implies that whitespace is insignificant, but in practice, ignoring it often leads to concatenated words. A safe strategy for LLM parsing is to treat all text as significant unless explicitly known otherwise.

Standard parsing libraries often extract all visible text, including content that has been crossed out. For an LLM, consuming retracted information as fact is a hallucination risk. The requirements mandate treating static strikethrough as deprecated content.
Strikethrough is defined in the Run Properties (<w:rPr>).

Defined by <w:dstrike w:val="true"/>. The parser must inspect the rPr of every run. If either element is present and active (not false), the ignore_text flag in the current context must be set to True. Any <w:t> elements encountered while this flag is active are discarded or moved to a separate "deleted_content" metadata bucket, ensuring they do not pollute the primary text stream.
Distinct from static formatting, tracked changes use specific container elements:
<w:del>: Represents content deleted with Track Changes enabled. This content should invariably be ignored.
<w:ins>: Represents content inserted. This content should invariably be included.
<w:moveFrom> and <w:moveTo>: Represent moved content. The parser must include moveTo and ignore moveFrom to reflect the final document state.

Japanese documents utilize vertical text layout for specific semantic contexts (formal reports, novels, postcards). While the underlying XML storage order of text usually remains logical (reading order), the layout implications are significant for LLMs attempting spatial reasoning.
Vertical alignment is controlled by the <w:textDirection> element, found in Paragraph Properties (<w:pPr>) or Table Cell Properties (<w:tcPr>).
The typical value for Japanese vertical text is tbRl (Top-to-Bottom, Right-to-Left).

When the parser detects <w:textDirection w:val="tbRl"/>, it should inject a metadata token (e.g., ``) into the extracted text block. This provides the LLM with context regarding the document's structure, distinguishing it from standard horizontal text which implies a different hierarchy of headers and footers.
5. Japanese Semantic Parsing: The Ruby (Furigana) Challenge

A critical deficiency in standard English-centric parsers is the mishandling of Ruby annotations—small phonetic guides (usually Hiragana) placed above Kanji characters to aid pronunciation. In XML, this is represented by the <w:ruby> element. Naive extraction concatenates the base text and the ruby text (e.g., parsing "Tokyo" and "Toukyou" as "Tokyotoukyou"), which destroys the tokenization capability of the LLM.
The <w:ruby> element encapsulates the entire construct and typically contains three children:
The parser must handle two distinct structural variations identified in the research:
Each Kanji character is explicitly paired with its reading in a separate <w:ruby> object. This is verbose but granular.

A sequence of Kanji characters is grouped together, and the reading is provided as a single string distributed across them. In XML, this appears as a <w:rubyBase> containing multiple text runs, paired with a single <w:rt>.
The parser must linearize this structure into a format that preserves semantics without introducing noise.
Push RubyContext to stack. Initialize buffers for base_text and ruby_text.
Capture the text content into the ruby_text buffer.
Capture the text content into the base_text buffer. Note that rubyBase may contain multiple <w:r> elements (especially in Group Ruby); the parser must aggregate all of them.
Output base_text only (e.g., "Tokyo"). This is often preferred for LLMs as the Kanji is semantically richer and the phonetic guide is redundant for a model trained on Japanese tokens.
Output base_text(ruby_text) (e.g., "Tokyo(Toukyou)"). This should be configurable, as it is valuable for disambiguating rare names but noisy for common words.

The <w:rubyAlign> attribute (values: distributeSpace, center, left, right) affects visual rendering but not the semantic content. For text extraction, this attribute can generally be ignored, though recording it as metadata helps reconstruct the visual fidelity if necessary.

DOCX numbering is one of the most complex subsystems to parse because the visual number (e.g., "1.2.a") is not stored in the document.xml content. It is generated dynamically at runtime through a triple-indirection mechanism involving the paragraph, the numbering instance, and the abstract numbering definition.
To reconstruct the list marker, the parser must implement a state machine that persists across the document stream.
A paragraph is identified as a list item if its properties <w:pPr> contain a <w:numPr> element.
<w:numId w:val="X"/>: References a numbering definition instance.
<w:ilvl w:val="Y"/>: Specifies the indentation level (0-based depth).

The parser looks up the numId="X" in the numbering.xml part. This element, <w:num>, points to an abstract numbering ID via <w:abstractNumId w:val="Z"/>. Crucially, the <w:num> element may contain <w:lvlOverride> elements. These overrides reset the starting value of a specific level (e.g., restarting a list at "1" after an interruption). The parser must check for overrides
The parser consults the <w:abstractNum w:abstractNumId="Z">. This definition contains the formatting rules for each level (<w:lvl>).
The starting value of the counter.
The text pattern. For example, %1.%2. implies "Level 1 counter, dot, Level 2 counter, dot".
The parser must maintain a dictionary mapping numId to a list of current counter values (one for each level).
Increment the counter for the current ilvl.
Reset all counters for levels deeper than ilvl to their start values (usually 1).
Generate the string by substituting the current counter values into the lvlText pattern.
Insert the generated string (e.g., "1.2.") at the beginning of the paragraph text sent to the LLM.

Tables in OOXML are row-based (<w:tr>) containers of cells (<w:tc>). However, unlike HTML tables, OOXML tables rely on a "Table Grid" definition to handle column alignment and merging, which complicates extraction.

A cell that spans multiple columns uses the <w:gridSpan w:val="N"/> element. The parser must effectively "repeat" this cell or insert placeholders for the next N-1 logical columns to maintain alignment in the text output.
val="restart": Indicates the start of a vertically merged region.
val="continue" (or empty): Indicates this cell is a continuation of the merge from the row above.

To resolve vertical merges in a streaming parser, the system must cache the content or state of the "active" merge for each column index. When a "continue" tag is encountered, the parser refers to the cached content from the initiating row. For LLM output, it is often semantically clearer to de-merge these cells—repeating the content in each cell—so the model understands the data applies to that specific row context.

Tables can exist inside table cells. The Context Stack architecture handles this naturally: encountering a <w:tbl> inside a <w:tc> simply pushes a new TableContext. The parser must ensure that row/column counters for the inner table do not interfere with the outer table's state.
Images are stored in the word/media/ folder, but their semantic value lies in the XML markup linking them to the text.
Images are referenced via the <w:drawing> element. This element contains a reference ID (e.g., r:embed="rIdX"). The parser must:
Resolve the rId in the _rels map to find the binary file path.
Read the binary data from the ZIP stream.
Generate a token in the text stream (e.g., ``) to preserve the position of the image relative to the text.
Accessibility data is crucial for multimodal LLM tasks. This data is found in the
descr: Description. Typically holds the "Alt Text".
title: Title. Often used for short captions.

Research indicates inconsistency across Word versions. Older "Strict" variants might populate descr but not title, or vice versa. The parser should implement a fallback strategy: check title, then descr, or concatenate both if present to ensure maximum semantic context is captured.
Word documents frequently use "Fields" for dynamic content like hyperlinks, dates, and tables of contents. These are represented by a complex state machine in XML.
A complex field is a sequence of runs delimited by <w:fldChar> elements.
Marks the start of the field.

The parser must detect the begin state, buffer the instrText to extract the URL via regex, wait for the separate marker, then buffer the result text. On end, it should output a structured link object: [LINK: "Click Here" -> "http://example.com"].

The w:dirty="true" attribute on a field character indicates the field value is stale and needs refreshing. The parser should capture this flag as metadata, warning the LLM that the text content (e.g., a "Total Cost" calculation) might be outdated.

Utilizing python-docx limits the developer to that library's interpretation of the DOM. It often discards "unknown" elements (like complex Ruby tags or new semantic tags) and forces a full memory load. A raw XML parsing strategy using lxml or standard xml.etree provides the granular control necessary to implement the specific Japanese handling and deprecation logic defined above.
The parser must ingest styles.xml at initialization. This file defines the default properties (<w:docDefaults>) and named styles.
Styles inherit from other styles via <w:basedOn>. The parser must resolve this chain to determine the final properties of a paragraph (e.g., is it bold? is it a header?).

Paragraph properties (pPr) override Style properties, which override Document Defaults. The parser must compute this "effective style" for accurate metadata extraction (e.g., identifying "Heading 1" even if manual formatting was applied).
The following Python structures are recommended for the implementation:
Dictionary mapping prefixes (w, wp) to full URIs.
Dictionary mapping rId to target file paths.
Dictionary mapping numId to current counter states.
Directed graph representing style inheritance for property resolution.
List of state objects tracking current XML depth and active flags (strikethrough, table cell, ruby).

By adhering to this specification, the resulting parser will be capable of transforming complex, Japanese-heavy DOCX archives into clean, semantically rich, and structured text streams optimized for high-performance LLM ingestion.
